sections.title: Introduction to Direct Object Pronouns
sections.content: Direct object pronouns (COD - complément d'objet direct) replace the direct object of a verb (the person or thing directly receiving the action). They answer the question 'what?' or 'whom?' after the verb.
examples: Je vois le chat → Je le vois (I see the cat → I see it)
Tu regardes la télé → Tu la regardes (You watch TV → You watch it)
Il achète des fleurs → Il en achète (He buys flowers → He buys some)

sections.title: Direct Object Pronouns
sections.content: The direct object pronouns are: me, te, le, la, nous, vous, les. They replace specific direct objects and must agree in gender and number.
examples: le → replaces masculine singular: Je vois le livre → Je le vois
la → replaces feminine singular: Je vois la voiture → Je la vois
les → replaces plural: Je vois les livres → Je les vois
me → replaces me (direct object): Tu vois moi → Tu me vois
te → replaces you: Je vois toi → Je te vois

sections.title: Placement with Conjugated Verbs
sections.content: With conjugated verbs, direct object pronouns come BEFORE the verb (except in affirmative imperative commands).
examples: Je le vois (I see it)
Tu la manges (You eat it)
Il nous regarde (He watches us)
Nous vous appelons (We call you)
Ils les achètent (They buy them)

sections.title: Placement with Infinitives
sections.content: When the verb is in the infinitive (after another verb), the direct object pronoun can come either before the conjugated verb or after the infinitive.
examples: Je veux le voir (I want to see it) - before conjugated verb
Je veux voir le chat → Je veux le voir OR Je veux voir le
Il peut la finirs (He can finish it) - before conjugated verb
Il peut finir la → less common

sections.title: Y as Direct Object Pronoun
sections.content: Y replaces a place or thing introduced by 'à' (indirect object) but can also replace certain direct objects referring to places.
examples: Je vais à Paris → J'y vais (I go to Paris → I go there)
Tu penses à ton travail → Tu y penses (You think about your work → You think about it)

sections.title: En as Direct Object Pronoun
sections.content: En replaces a direct object introduced by 'de' (partitive or quantity). It can mean 'some', 'any', or refer to a quantity.
examples: Je veux du pain → J'en veux (I want some bread → I want some)
Tu as des livres → Tu en as (You have books → You have some)
Il a trois pommes → Il en a trois (He has three apples → He has three of them)

sections.title: Negation with Direct Object Pronouns
sections.content: In negation, the structure is: ne + pronoun + verb + pas. The pronoun comes before the verb.
examples: Je ne le vois pas (I don't see it)
Tu ne la manges pas (You don't eat it)
Il ne nous appelle pas (He doesn't call us)

sections.title: Practice Exercise
sections.content: Rewrite these sentences replacing the direct objects with pronouns: 1) Je vois Marie. 2) Tu achètes des fleurs. 3) Il regarde la télévision. 4) Nous appelons nos amis. 5) Vous attendez le bus.
